title: How the dismissal of Ukraine’s popular defence minister backfired for Zelenskyy
gist: Zelenskyy's dismissal of popular defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov — architect of Ukraine's tech-first drone strategy and anti-corruption procurement reforms — sparked nationwide protests and a political crisis, forcing a partial reversal (sacking rival commander Syrskyi) that reads as a pattern of the president sidelining capable rivals ahead of possible autumn elections.

insights.claim: Ukraine's popular defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov, architect of the country's successful drone campaign against Russia, was stripped of his role after only six months in office, triggering protests across Ukraine demanding his reinstatement.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Political instability at the top of Ukraine's defence establishment is a risk signal for anyone tracking the pace of Ukraine's drone/defence-tech output and Western co-production deals.
insights.quote: The popular defence minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, was stripped of his role after only six months in office.
insights.locator: For the second time this year

insights.claim: Under protest pressure Zelenskyy sacked Commander-in-Chief Oleksandr Syrskyi and replaced him with joint forces chief Mykhailo Drapatyi, an ally of Fedorov, signalling a partial reversal to defuse the crisis.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Personnel churn at commander-in-chief level within one crisis shows how quickly Ukraine's top military leadership can shift, affecting continuity of operations and reform.
insights.quote: Syrskyi has now been sacked and replaced with joint forces chief Mykhailo Drapatyi , seen as an ally of Fedorov.

insights.claim: The Fedorov–Syrskyi feud reflects a doctrinal clash between an archaic Soviet-style approach prioritizing artillery, ground infantry and high casualty tolerance, and an asymmetric tech-driven model designed to outsmart a larger adversary.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The winner of this doctrinal fight determines whether Ukraine's demand tilts toward legacy artillery/manpower or scalable drone and software systems — a directional signal for defence suppliers.
insights.quote: Fedorov champions an asymmetric, tech-driven approach to warfare designed to outsmart a larger adversary.
insights.locator: Competing visions of war and governance

insights.claim: Fedorov's concrete track record included scaling domestic drone production, negotiating the shutdown of Starlink terminals used illegally by Russian forces, and introducing 'after action reviews' to boost Ukraine's missile interception rates.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: These are the specific capability gains at risk of stalling under new leadership; watch interception rates and domestic drone output as proxies for whether reforms survive.
insights.quote: His track record includes scaling domestic drone production, negotiating the shutdown of Starlink terminals used illegally by Russian forces, and introducing ‘after action reviews’ (AAR) to boost Ukraine’s missile interception rates.

insights.claim: Fedorov's introduction of competitive tendering and digitized procurement tracking disrupted an opaque defence supply system long exploited by corrupt generals to inflate contracts and hide kickbacks, creating powerful vested interests opposed to his reforms.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Entrenched defence-industry corruption is a structural headwind to reform; the protests partly pit reformers against contractors losing rigged contracts.
insights.quote: His introduction of competitive tendering and digitized procurement tracking made it increasingly difficult to inflate supply contracts and hide illicit kickbacks.

insights.claim: Public trust in Fedorov surged from 38 per cent to 50 per cent over six months while trust in Zelenskyy stayed flat at 61-62 per cent, per a Kyiv Institute of International Sociology survey, signalling a rising rival even though Fedorov has never claimed presidential ambitions.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Fedorov's rising political capital is the likeliest true motive for his removal and a variable to track for post-war Ukrainian leadership scenarios.
insights.quote: public trust in Fedorov surged from 38 per cent to 50 per cent over the last six months, whereas trust in Zelenskyy remained stable at around 61-62 per cent
insights.locator: While Fedorov has never publicly expressed presidential ambitions

insights.claim: A May 2026 poll indicated 67 per cent of Ukrainians expect Zelenskyy to be replaced once the war ends, even though elections are constitutionally barred under martial law.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Broad expectation of leadership change post-war frames every current move as pre-election positioning; a ceasefire could trigger rapid political transition.
insights.quote: a May 2026 poll indicated that 67 per cent of Ukrainians expect Zelenskyy to be replaced once the war ends

insights.claim: Sidelining capable and popular officials through dismissal or co-optation has become a distinct pattern of Zelenskyy's leadership, exemplified by dismissing Zaluzhnyi to ambassador to the UK around the timing of would-be presidential elections and appointing Budanov to restore trust after the Yermak corruption scandal.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A recurring rival-neutralization pattern makes Ukrainian governance increasingly personalized and less predictable — a governance-risk flag amid Western pressure for democratic elections.
insights.quote: sidelining capable and popular officials has become a distinct pattern of Zelenskyy’s leadership style

insights.claim: Intelligence chief Kyrylo Budanov consistently ranks among the top three most trusted Ukrainian political figures, with late-2025 data suggesting he could defeat Zelenskyy in a hypothetical runoff.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Budanov is an underappreciated contender in post-war leadership scenarios worth tracking alongside Zaluzhnyi and Fedorov.
insights.quote: data in late 2025 suggesting he could defeat Zelenskyy in a hypothetical runoff election

insights.claim: Fedorov's dismissal came mid-operation: his 40-day campaign to isolate Russian-occupied Crimea had reached unprecedented momentum, and bureaucratic slowdowns could now leave frontline brigades vulnerable as Russia prepares another mobilization wave after its autumn Duma elections.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Leadership disruption during an active Crimea-isolation operation is a near-term battlefield risk; watch for stalled momentum and Russian mobilization timing.
insights.quote: Fedorov’s 40-day operation to isolate the Russian-occupied Crimean Peninsula had reached unprecedented momentum
insights.locator: The timing of Zelenskyy’s decision is particularly baffling

insights.claim: Ukraine had just signed the EU-Ukraine Defence Industrial Partnership for joint production and integration of combat-tested drone and counter-drone tech with European manufacturing, and Fedorov had applied to route EU funds toward US and European anti-ballistic missile technology to address air-defence shortages.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: European defence manufacturers and anti-ballistic missile suppliers have direct exposure to whether these Fedorov-driven partnerships and fund flows continue under new leadership.
insights.quote: Ukraine had just signed the EU-Ukraine Defence Industrial Partnership, establishing a framework for joint production and integration of combat-tested drone and counter-drone technologies with European manufacturing capacity.

insights.claim: EU Defence Commissioner Andrius Kubilius publicly called Fedorov's sudden removal a 'big surprise', emphasizing he was the driver of Ukraine's tech-first, asymmetric drone strategy.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Open EU-level dismay signals friction between Kyiv's internal politics and Western defence-industrial partners underwriting Ukraine's capabilities.
insights.quote: EU Defence Commissioner Andrius Kubilius, meanwhile, called Fedorov’s sudden removal a ‘ big surprise ’
